fact: November 4 is exactly 308 days into the year, which means there are only 57 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for squirrels to go absolutely nuts burying acorns - they can bury up to 10,000 acorns in a single season!
The sun sets before 5 PM in most northern cities around this date, making it feel like bedtime when you're just getting home from school.
November 4 falls smack in the middle of Scorpio season - the zodiac sign known for being mysterious and intense, just like autumn itself.
In deciduous forests, this is when trees are basically having a massive leaf-dropping party, with some trees losing 200,000 leaves each!
Arctic foxes in northern regions are busy growing their thick winter coats right now, which can be 200% thicker than their summer fur.
The date sits almost perfectly between the autumn equinox and winter solstice - it's like the exact middle of fall's countdown to winter.
In many places, morning frost starts appearing regularly around this time, creating those magical sparkly lawns that crunch under your feet.
Monarch butterflies in North America are deep into their epic 3,000-mile migration to Mexico, flying up to 100 miles per day.
The moon phase around November 4 varies each year, but when it's a new moon, the lack of moonlight makes this one of the darkest times of the year.
In the Southern Hemisphere, November 4 is actually spring cleaning time for nature - flowers are blooming and baby animals are being born everywhere.
This is peak season for seeing your breath in cold air - that misty puff happens when the temperature drops below about 45°F and your warm breath hits the cold air.
